---
title: "Untitled"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r}
#Credit Score Model
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(corrplot)
library(splines)
library(vip)    #variable importance
library(rpart.plot)
library(xgboost)
library(themis)
```


```{r}
# Read the data files
data <- read_csv("GiveMeSomeCredit/cs-training.csv",show_col_types = FALSE)

#checking dimensions and glimpse for data
dim(data)
glimpse(data)

#checking Target variable
table(data$SeriousDlqin2yrs)

```

```{r}
# Removing ID column
data <- data[, -1]
glimpse(data)

```
```{r}
# Working on NA's
print("-------------------------------------------------------------")  
print("CHECKING ON NA's")
colSums(is.na(data))

#Doing Median Imputation for Montly income missing values
data<-data|>
      mutate(
      MonthlyIncome=ifelse(is.na(MonthlyIncome),
      median(MonthlyIncome,na.rm=TRUE),
      MonthlyIncome),
      
      NumberOfDependents=ifelse(is.na(NumberOfDependents),
      median(NumberOfDependents,na.rm=TRUE),
      NumberOfDependents)
      )
  
print("-------------------------------------------------------------")  
print("AFTER MEDIAN IMPUTATION")    
colSums(is.na(data))
```
```{#Step 2: Exploratory Data Analysis}

#2.1 Target Distribution  
#2.2 Age vs Default  
#2.3 Income vs Default  
#2.4 Debt Ratio vs Default  
#2.5 Correlation Analysis  

```

```{r}
# 2.1 Target Distribution

#Count values
data|>count(SeriousDlqin2yrs)|>
  mutate(percent=n/sum(n)*100)


```
Observation:93% are non defaulters.
6.7% are defaulters.
There is a class imbalance problem here.



```{r}
#2.2 Age vs Default 
data|> ggplot(aes(x=age,fill=factor(SeriousDlqin2yrs)))+
      geom_histogram(bins=30,alpha=0.6,position="identity")+
      labs(
        title="Age distribution by Default",
        x="Age",
        fill="Default(1=Yes)"
      )
```

Observation:Younger individuals tend to show a higher proportion of defaults compared to older individuals, indicating that age may be an important predictor of credit risk

```{r}
#2.3 Income vs Default
#We will use log transformation here, since income has huge variation

#HISTOGRAM
data|>
  ggplot(aes(x=log(MonthlyIncome+1),fill=factor(SeriousDlqin2yrs)))+
           geom_histogram(bins=30,alpha=0.6,position="identity")+
      labs(
        title="Income vs Default (Log Scale)",
        x="Income (log)",
        fill="Default(1=Yes)"
      )



#BOXPLOTS
data|>
  ggplot(aes(x=factor(SeriousDlqin2yrs),y=MonthlyIncome)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill="lightgreen")+
  labs(
    x = "Default (0 = No, 1 = Yes)",
    y = "Monthly Income",
    title = "Income vs Default"
  )

```
```{r}
#Now checking on defaulter rate on income groups
data|>
  mutate(income_group=ntile(MonthlyIncome,5)) |>
  group_by(income_group)|>
  summarise(default_rate=mean(SeriousDlqin2yrs)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x=factor(income_group),y=default_rate))+
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    x = "Income Group (Low → High)",
    y = "Default Rate",
    title = "Default Rate by Income Group"
  )

```
Observation:The data was divided into income groups using quantiles, and the default rate was calculated for each group. The results indicate that lower-income groups tend to have higher default rates compared to higher-income groups, suggesting income is a key determinant of credit risk.

```{r}
#2.4 Debt Ratio vs Default 

data |>
  mutate(debt_group = ntile(DebtRatio, 5)) |>
  group_by(debt_group) |>
  summarise(default_rate = mean(SeriousDlqin2yrs)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(debt_group), y = default_rate)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkred") +
  labs(
    x = "Debt Group (Low → High)",
    y = "Default Rate",
    title = "Default Rate by Debt Ratio"
  )

```
Observation:The relationship between debt ratio and default risk is non-linear. While higher debt generally increases default risk, the highest debt group exhibits a lower default rate, possibly due to higher income or better financial management among these individuals.
```{r}
#Analysing Group 5 (highest debt group)

data_grp <-data|>
  mutate(debt_group=ntile(DebtRatio,5))

#Filter only group 5
group5<- data_grp|>
  filter(debt_group==5)

#Check Debt Ratio Range

print("_________________________DEBT RATIO GROUP 5_______________________________________")
summary(group5$DebtRatio)
quantile(group5$DebtRatio, probs = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1))

#Monthly Income
print("_________________________MONTHLY INCOME GROUP 5____________________________________")
summary(group5$MonthlyIncome)

#Compare with overall data
print("_________________________MONTHLY INCOME OVERALL____________________________________")
summary(data$MonthlyIncome)

#Comparison beyween incomes (mean and median) between date groups
print("_________________________INCOME ACROSS GROUP________________________________________")
data_grp |>
  group_by(debt_group) |>
  summarise(
    avg_income = mean(MonthlyIncome, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_income = median(MonthlyIncome, na.rm = TRUE)
  )


```
Observation:
Mean > Median ==> Income is skewed (outliers present)
Median income of group 4 is low, so more defaulters.
Due to the skewed nature of income data, median values provide a more robust measure than mean. The results reveal that the fourth debt group exhibits the lowest median income and the highest default rate, suggesting that this group represents individuals with significant financial strain. The highest debt group does not have the lowest median income, indicating that extreme debt ratios may be influenced by factors such as very low income rather than true financial burden.

```{r}
#2.5 Correlation Analysis 
#How variables are related to each other

#co-relation matrix
cor_mat <- cor(data)

#Visualize (Heatmap)
corrplot(cor_mat, method = "color", tl.cex = 0.6)


```
Observation:
Strong correlation: These are very important predictors
NumberOfTimes90DaysLate
NumberOfTime30-59DaysPastDueNotWorse
NumberOfTime60-89DaysPastDueNotWorse

Moderate correlation:
Number of Dependants

Low coorelation:
Age (usually weaker)


Correlation analysis reveals that past delinquency variables are strongly associated with default risk, highlighting their predictive importance. While some inter-variable correlations exist, they are not sufficiently high to cause multicollinearity concerns, suggesting that most variables can be retained for modeling


CONCLUSION FROM EDA:
Customers with lower income, higher debt burden, and past payment issues are more likely to default.
Class Imbalance: The dataset is highly imbalanced, with significantly fewer default cases compared to non-default cases. This is important for model evaluation.

Age Effect: Younger individuals tend to have a higher likelihood of default compared to older individuals, suggesting age is a relevant predictor.

Income Effect: Income is an important factor in credit risk. While raw distributions were skewed, analysis using log transformation and grouped comparisons indicates that lower-income individuals tend to exhibit higher default risk.

Debt Ratio Effect: Debt ratio shows a non-linear relationship with default risk. The highest default rate occurs in the fourth debt group, indicating that moderate-to-high debt levels combined with low income represent the highest risk segment.

Income vs Debt Insight: Analysis of income across debt groups reveals that higher debt levels are associated with lower income, confirming that financial stress increases when income is low and debt burden is high.

Median vs Mean Insight: Due to the skewed nature of income data, median values provided a more reliable measure than mean, highlighting the importance of robust statistics in financial data analysis.

Correlation Insight: Variables related to past delinquency exhibit strong relationships with default, indicating their importance in predictive modeling. No severe multicollinearity was observed.






```{r}
#Step 3: Model Building
#3.1 Data Preparation
  #Train-test split
  #Cross-validation (folds)
  #Preprocessing (recipe):
  #Missing values
  #Normalization
  #Splines (non-linearity)

#3.2 Baseline Model (Logistic Regression)
  #Build logistic regression
  #Fit model
  #Evaluate using:
  #ROC AUC
  #Accuracy

#3.3 Random Forest (Tuned Model)

#3.4 Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)

#3.5 Model Comparison
  #Compare ROC AUC
  #Select best model
```


```{r}
#3.1 Data Preparation

#Train-test split
#strata = SeriousDlqin2yrs keeps class balance similar in both sets

#Keep 1 = default as the positive class by making "1" the first factor level.
data<-data|>
  mutate(SeriousDlqin2yrs=factor(SeriousDlqin2yrs,levels=c("1","0")))

set.seed(123)
split<-initial_split(data,prop=0.8,strata=SeriousDlqin2yrs)
train<-training(split)
test<-testing(split)

dim(train)
dim(test)

#converting SeriousDlqin2yrs into factor so that it looks like classification problem in R
#train<-train|>
 # mutate(SeriousDlqin2yrs=factor(SeriousDlqin2yrs))

#test <- test |>
  #mutate(SeriousDlqin2yrs = factor(SeriousDlqin2yrs))

```
```{r}
#Cross-validation folds

set.seed(123)
folds<-vfold_cv(train,v=5,strata=SeriousDlqin2yrs)
folds
```


```{r}
#Preprocessing recipe
#Step:	What happens
#recipe:	defines steps
#prep:	learns parameters
#bake:	applies transformations

rec<-recipe(SeriousDlqin2yrs ~ . , data=train) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  #step_smote(SeriousDlqin2yrs)|>   # SMOTE
  step_cut(age, breaks = c(0,25,35,50,70,100)) |>
  step_ns(MonthlyIncome,deg_free = 3) |>
  step_ns(DebtRatio, deg_free = 3) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())


#Learn from data → prep()
#Apply transformation → bake()

prep_rec<-prep(rec)
train_processed <-bake(prep_rec,new_data=NULL)

glimpse(train_processed)

#check missing value after preprocessing
colSums(is.na(train_processed))


#length of folds counts
length(folds$splits)

```

Observation:The dataset was split into training and testing sets using an 80/20 split with stratification on the target variable to preserve class proportions. Five-fold cross-validation was then applied to the training set for robust model evaluation. A preprocessing recipe was created to handle missing values using median imputation, normalize numeric predictors, and incorporate spline transformations for age and monthly income to capture possible non-linear relationships

```{r}

#3.2 Baseline Model (Logistic Regression)

#Build logistic regression
log_model<-logistic_reg()|>
  set_engine("glm") |>
  set_mode("classification")

#Create workflow
log_wf<-workflow()|>
  add_recipe(rec)|>
  add_model(log_model)

#fit with cross validation
set.seed(123)

log_res<-fit_resamples(
  log_wf,
  resamples=folds,
  metrics=metric_set(roc_auc,
    accuracy,
    recall,
    sensitivity,
    specificity,
    precision,
    f_meas),
  control=control_resamples(save_pred=TRUE)
)

#performance
collect_metrics(log_res)


```
Observation on cross folds:

Model is correct 93% times
The dataset is imbalanced
Most people = non-default (0)
Few people = default (1)
So model can:
❌ Predict everything as 0
✔ Still get high accuracy

ROC AUC = 0.6995 (≈ 0.70)
AUC	Meaning
0.5	random
0.7	OK
0.8	good
0.9	excellent

Our model:~0.70 → OK but not strong

Although logistic regression achieves high accuracy, the relatively lower ROC AUC highlights its limitation in handling class imbalance and capturing non-linear relationships. Therefore, more advanced models such as tree-based methods are expected to provide better predictive performance.


```{r}

#Fit final model on full training data
log_fit<-fit(log_wf,data=train)

#Predict on test data
log_prob <- predict(log_fit,new_data = test, type="prob")
log_class <- predict(log_fit, new_data = test, type = "class")

# Combine predictions with true test data
log_results<-bind_cols(test,log_prob,log_class)
head(log_results)

# Evaluate on test data
roc_auc(log_results, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, .pred_1, event_level = "second")
accuracy(log_results,truth=SeriousDlqin2yrs, estimate=.pred_class)

# confusion matrix
conf_mat(log_results, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, estimate = .pred_class)

```
Observation on test data:
The logistic regression model achieves an ROC AUC of approximately 0.70, indicating moderate discriminatory power. While the model is able to rank defaulters higher than non-defaulters to some extent, the separation between classes remains limited, suggesting that more complex models may improve predictive performance.

```{r}
```


```{r}
#CURVES RELATED TO LOGISTIC REGRESSION
```


```{r}
#1.ROC Curve ==> Shows how well the model separates defaulters vs non-defaulters

roc_manual <- log_results |>
  arrange(desc(.pred_1)) |>
  mutate(
    TP = cumsum(SeriousDlqin2yrs == "1"),
    FP = cumsum(SeriousDlqin2yrs == "0"),
    FN = sum(SeriousDlqin2yrs == "1") - TP,
    TN = sum(SeriousDlqin2yrs == "0") - FP,
    TPR = TP / (TP + FN),
    FPR = FP / (FP + TN)
  )

ggplot(roc_manual, aes(x = FPR, y = TPR)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue") +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "ROC Curve (Manual)")




```
Observation:The ROC curve lies above the diagonal, indicating that the logistic regression model performs better than random classification. However, the gradual slope and lack of sharp curvature near the top-left corner suggest limited discriminative power. This indicates that while the model ranks observations reasonably well (AUC ≈ 0.69), it does not strongly separate the classes, likely due to underfitting or insufficient feature representation


```{r}
#2.Confusion Matrix Heatmap

cm <- conf_mat(log_results, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, estimate = .pred_class)
cm
autoplot(cm, type = "heatmap")

```
Observation:
TP = 81
FN = 1895

We are missing almost ALL defaulters
Sensitivity=TP(TP+FN)=0.041
Only 4.1% of defaulters are detected
This is a major failure in credit risk
Accuracy is misleading!

Accuracy= Total TP+TN≈veryhigh
dataset is heavily imbalanced
most people are non-defaulters


```{r}
#3.Histogram / Density of Predicted Probabilities by Actual Class
log_results |>
  ggplot(aes(x = .pred_1, fill = SeriousDlqin2yrs)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted Default Probability by Actual Class",
    x = "Predicted Probability of Default",
    fill = "Actual Class"
  )



```
Observation:
Both curves heavily overlap
Most values are between 0.00 – 0.15
Defaulters are slightly higher, but not clearly separated
our model is not separating the two classes well
Even though:Defaulters have slightly higher probabilities
The difference is too small.

The model is Weak separation + poor calibration.

```{r}
#Calibration Plot

#Shows whether predicted probabilities match actual observed risk.

calib <- log_results |>
  mutate(bin = ntile(.pred_1, 10)) |>
  group_by(bin) |>
  summarise(
    mean_pred = mean(.pred_1),
    obs_rate = mean(as.numeric(SeriousDlqin2yrs) - 1)
  )

ggplot(calib, aes(x = mean_pred, y = obs_rate)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Calibration Plot",
    x = "Average Predicted Probability",
    y = "Observed Default Rate"
  )


```
Observation:
The calibration plot suggests that the predicted probabilities are systematically lower than the observed default rates, indicating poor calibration. The model underestimates the likelihood of default, which is problematic in credit risk applications. Although the model shows reasonable ranking ability (as seen from ROC), its probability estimates are unreliable and require recalibration or improved feature representation.



FINAL CONCLUSION ON LOGISTIC REGRESSION:
The logistic regression model demonstrates moderate discriminative ability with an AUC of approximately 0.69, indicating it can reasonably rank defaulters above non-defaulters. However, the confusion matrix reveals very poor recall (~4%), meaning the model fails to identify most defaulters. The predicted probability distributions show significant overlap between classes, with most values concentrated at low levels, indicating weak separation. Additionally, calibration analysis suggests that the model underestimates default risk. Overall, while the model performs adequately in ranking, it is not effective for classification or decision-making in its current form due to class imbalance, low sensitivity, and limited feature representation.








#3.4 Random Forest (Tuned Model)
#captures non-linear patterns
#handles feature interactions automatically
#works well with imbalanced data

  ##Build Random Forest
  ##Fit model (with tuning + workflow)
  ###Select best model
  ##Evaluate using: ROC AUC,Accuracy
  ##Graphs
```{r}

#Build model

#receipe with SMOTE to handle class inbalance

rf_rec <- recipe(SeriousDlqin2yrs ~ ., data = train) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_smote(SeriousDlqin2yrs)

rf_model<-rand_forest(
  trees=500,
  mtry=tune(),
  min_n=tune()) |>
  set_engine("ranger") |>
  set_mode("classification")


#create workflow
rf_wf<-workflow() |>
  add_recipe(rf_rec) |>
  add_model(rf_model)


#Tune model
set.seed(123)

rf_res<-tune_grid(
  rf_wf,
  resamples=folds,
  grid=5,
  metrics=metric_set(roc_auc,accuracy),
  control = control_grid(verbose = TRUE)
)

#Check performance
collect_metrics(rf_res)

#select best parameters
best_rf<-select_best(rf_res,metric="roc_auc")
best_rf


#Finalize model
final_rf<-finalize_workflow(rf_wf,best_rf)

#Fit on full training data
rf_fit<-fit(final_rf,data=train)

#Predict on test data
rf_pred <- predict(rf_fit, test, type = "prob") |>
  bind_cols(predict(rf_fit, test)) |>
  bind_cols(test)

#ROC AUC
roc_auc(rf_pred, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, .pred_1)

#accuracy
accuracy(rf_pred, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, .pred_class)


```
Observation:After finalizing and fitting on the full training set, your test set results became:
ROC AUC = 0.8534:It means the model has good ability to separate defaulters vs non-defaulters,the model will assign a higher risk score to the defaulter about 85.3% of the time
Accuracy = 0.9370 :About 93.7% of cases were classified correctly.



```{r}
#CURVES RELATED TO RANDOM FOREST:

#1. ROC CURVE
library(yardstick)

roc_curve(rf_pred, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, .pred_1) |>
  autoplot()
```
Observation: 
X-axis:1−specificity → False Positive Rate
Y-axis: Sensitivity → True Positive Rate
Dotted diagonal line → Random model (AUC = 0.5)
curve (black line) →  Random Forest model
Strongly above the diagonal
Bends quickly toward top-left corner

This means:Model is good at distinguishing defaulters vs non-defaulters

```{r}
#2.Precision-Recall Curve:When we predict default → how often we are  correct?”
pr_curve(rf_pred, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, .pred_1) |>
  autoplot()
```
Observation:
X-axis (Recall) → How many defaulters we catch
Y-axis (Precision) → Out of predicted defaulters, how many are actually defaulters

Left side (start of curve)
Recall is low (we are catching only a few defaulters)
Precision is high (~80%)
We are very accurate, but only catching a small number of defaulters

Middle of curve
Recall increases (we catch more defaulters)
Precision drops (~40–50%)
We catch more defaulters, but also start making more mistakes.

Right side (end of curve)
Recall ≈ 1 (you catch almost ALL defaulters)
Precision is very low (~10%)
We are catching everyone, but many are NOT defaulters

we label almost everyone as risky → lots of wrong predictions

Best strategy is:Focus on left side of curve

“Pick only high-risk customers where precision is high”

Because: Banks don’t want to wrongly reject good customers

```{r}
#3. Confusion Matrix

conf_mat(rf_pred,truth=SeriousDlqin2yrs,estimate = .pred_class)
```
We are missing many defaulters:

1842 defaulters missed
Only 137 caught



FINAL CONCLUSION ON RANDOM FOREST
The model is conservative, favoring safe predictions and minimizing false alarms, but at the cost of missing many high-risk customers.
#we can work on classification threshold

The Random Forest model was trained using cross-validation and optimized based on ROC AUC. The best hyperparameters selected were mtry = 1 and min_n = 11, resulting in strong overall performance.

On the test data:

ROC AUC ≈ 0.85 → good ability to distinguish defaulters vs non-defaulters
Accuracy ≈ 93.7% → high overall classification performance

However, deeper evaluation using the confusion matrix and Precision–Recall curve reveals important insights:

The model performs well in identifying non-defaulters
It produces very few false positives (good customers wrongly flagged)
But it misses a large number of defaulters (high false negatives)

The Random Forest model demonstrates strong ranking ability (AUC ~0.85), but requires threshold tuning to better capture defaulters, as it currently prioritizes overall accuracy over risk detection.




#3.3 Decision Tree (Tuned Model)
#Captures non-linearity

  ##Build Decision Tree
  ##Fit model (with tuning + workflow)
  ###Select best model
  ##Evaluate using: ROC AUC,Accuracy
  ##Graphs
```{r}
#Build Decision Tree model
dt_model<-decision_tree(
  cost_complexity = tune(),
  tree_depth=tune(),
  min_n=tune()
)|>
  set_engine("rpart") |>
  set_mode("classification")

#create workflow
dt_wf<-workflow()|>
  add_recipe(rec)|>
  add_model(dt_model)

#tune model
set.seed(123)
dt_res<-tune_grid(
  dt_wf,
  resamples=folds,
  grid=10,
  metrics=metric_set(roc_auc,accuracy),
  control=control_grid(verbose=TRUE)
)

#Check Performance
collect_metrics(dt_res)

#Select best model
best_dt<-select_best(dt_res,metric="roc_auc")
best_dt

#finalise and fit models
final_dt <- finalize_workflow(dt_wf, best_dt)
dt_fit <- fit(final_dt, data = train)

#Predict on Test Data
dt_pred<-predict(dt_fit,test,type="prob") |>
  bind_cols(predict(dt_fit,test)) |>
  bind_cols(test)

#Evaluate Model
roc_auc(dt_pred, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, .pred_1)
accuracy(dt_pred, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, .pred_class)


  

```


```{r}
#plot tree (although this is not useful)
dt_fit |>
  extract_fit_parsnip() |>
  pluck("fit") |>
  rpart.plot()
```


```{r}
#confusion matrix
conf_mat(dt_pred, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, estimate = .pred_class)
```
Observation: Decision Tree is“More aggressive”
Flags more people as risky (compared to Random Forest)
Catches more defaulters(compared to Random Forest)
But also mislabels more safe customers(compared to Random Forest)
```{r}
#2.Precision-Recall Curve:When we predict default → how often we are  correct?”
pr_curve(dt_pred, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, .pred_1) |>
  autoplot()
```
Observation:“Finds more risky people, but makes more mistakes”
Recall (X-axis) → how many defaulters you catch
Precision (Y-axis) → how accurate those predictions are

Random Forest PR curve (earlier):
Precision at start: ~75–80%
Decision Tree PR curve:
Precision at start: ~55–60%
Random Forest is more precise at top predictions
Decision Tree:Catches more defaulters (higher recall),But less accurate

DECISION TREE SUMMARY
The Decision Tree model shows moderate performance with lower precision compared to Random Forest, but captures a higher number of defaulters, highlighting the trade-off between recall and precision in imbalanced classification problems.



#3.4 XGBoost (Tuned Model)
#Captures complex patterns + improves performance over Random Forest

  # Data Preparation
  # XGBoost Model Specification
  # Workflow Construction
  # Cross-Validation Results
  # Final Model Fit
  # Test Set Evaluation
  # Feature Importance
```{r}

# recipe
xgb_rec <- recipe(SeriousDlqin2yrs ~ ., data = train) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_smote(SeriousDlqin2yrs) |>
  step_cut(age, breaks = c(0, 25, 35, 50, 70, 120)) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_zv(all_predictors())

# tunable xgboost model
xgb_tune <- boost_tree(
  trees = 500,
  tree_depth = tune(),
  learn_rate = tune(),
  min_n = tune(),
  loss_reduction = 0,
  sample_size = 0.8
) |>
  set_engine("xgboost") |>
  set_mode("classification")

# create workflow
xgb_tuned_wf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(xgb_rec) |>
  add_model(xgb_tune)

# define grid
xgb_grid <- grid_regular(
  tree_depth(range = c(3L, 10L)),
  learn_rate(range = c(-3, -1)),
  min_n(range = c(5L, 30L)),
  levels = 3
)

# tune with cross-validation
set.seed(123)
xgb_res <- tune_grid(
  xgb_tuned_wf,
  resamples = folds,
  grid = xgb_grid,
  metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy, sens, spec,recall, precision, f_meas),
  control = control_grid(save_pred = TRUE)
)

collect_metrics(xgb_res)
show_best(xgb_res, metric = "roc_auc")

```


```{r}
#Fit final model on training data
best_params<-select_best(xgb_res,metric="roc_auc")
final_xgb_wf<-finalize_workflow(xgb_tuned_wf,best_params)
xgb_fit<- fit(final_xgb_wf,data=train)

#Predict on test_set (class)
test_pred_class<-predict(xgb_fit,new_data=test,type='class')

#Predict on test_set (probability)
test_pred_prob<-predict(xgb_fit,new_data=test,type='prob')

#combine above two

test_results<-bind_cols(test,test_pred_class,test_pred_prob)

glimpse(test_results)


```

```{r}

#evaluate
roc_auc(test_results, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, .pred_1)
accuracy(test_results, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, estimate = .pred_class)
conf_mat(test_results, truth = SeriousDlqin2yrs, estimate = .pred_class)


```
Observation:
Risky customers caught:
Recall(Sensitivity): 380/(380+1599)=19.2%  (Model misses nearly 80% of defaults)

How correct is the model when it predicts risky:
Precision :380/(380+257) =59.7%

Specificity: 27764/(27764+257) =99.1%

The binary classification model achieved strong performance with ROC AUC = 0.860, indicating good ability to distinguish risky and non-risky customers.
Overall accuracy = 93.81%, showing high prediction performance.
The model performs very well in identifying non-default / low-risk customers.
Sensitivity for detecting default customers is relatively low, meaning some risky customers are missed.
The model is conservative, with fewer false positives but higher false negatives.
Suitable as a credit scoring / customer ranking model.
Further improvement can be made through threshold tuning, class balancing, and recall optimization.
Overall, the model provides a strong base for credit risk decision-making.



The models were generally effective at identifying low-risk / non-default customers, but weaker in detecting default customers due to class imbalance.
This suggests the limitation may be less about the algorithms themselves and more about the data imbalance, feature quality, and decision threshold settings.
Since ROC AUC is reasonably strong, the models still have useful ranking power, meaning they can separate higher-risk from lower-risk customers.
Therefore, it is not that the models failed completely; rather, they require further optimization to become production-ready.
Better results may be achieved through:
Threshold tuning
Resampling methods (SMOTE / undersampling)
Feature engineering
Cost-sensitive learning
Ensemble / boosting methods
Final conclusion: None of the three models gave the best final solution yet, but they provide a solid baseline for improvement

```{r}

```
